chapters.title: From Omaha to a Fractured Childhood
chapters.text:
On May 19, 1925, Malcolm Little was born in an Omaha hospital, the seventh of Louise and Earl Little's nine children. His father, a Baptist minister, and the family faced constant harassment from Ku Klux Klan riders because they were African American. In 1931, when Malcolm was six, Earl was assassinated. Louise, then thirty-four, was left alone to raise the children.

Financial pressure mounted. She bought on credit, took domestic jobs with white families, and was repeatedly fired once employers discovered she was African American — easy to overlook because her straight black hair, fair complexion, and accent led many to assume she was white.

Losing a spouse isn't easy because it leaves only one person to bear the whole family's responsibility.

In 1937 welfare authorities split the family after Louise suffered a breakdown. Older siblings stayed in the home their father built; Malcolm, thirteen, went to the Gohannases while other children were adopted by families in Lansing. He was later sent to a detention home and reform school in Mason, where Mrs. Swerlin enrolled him in junior high as the only Black student in his class.

A 1940 visit to his sister Ella in Boston exposed him to Black life far different from Lansing and Mason. Returning to Mason, he felt increasingly alienated from the white world around him. After eighth grade he left for Boston to live with Ella.

chapters.title: Boston Street Life and Early Self-Destruction
chapters.text:
Ella advised Malcolm to explore Roxbury before job hunting. Walking Waumbeck and Humboldt Avenue Hill, he noticed African Americans imitating white manners and style. Shorty helped him land a shoe-shine job at the Roseland State Ballroom, then introduced him to credit spending, liquor, cigarettes, cannabis, and gambling. Lindy-hop dancing became an obsession he rarely missed.

Malcolm Little's first step to self-degradation was when he relaxed and dyed his hair to look like a white man's hair.

He quit shoe-shining for work at Townsend Drug Store, met Laura — a friendly reader whose parents had separated when she was a baby — and began lindy-hopping with her despite her grandmother's rules. At one dance he met Sophia, a white woman; their relationship boosted his status in Roxbury. He moved out of Ella's house, quit the drugstore, and waitered at the Parker House.

I had joined that multitude of Negro men and women in America who are brainwashed into believing that the black people are 'inferior' — and white people 'superior' — that they will even violate and mutilate their God-created bodies to try to look 'pretty' by white standards. ~ Malcolm X

chapters.title: Company You Keep Shapes the Life You Lead
chapters.text:
Rountree, an elderly Pullman porter and friend of Ella's, helped Malcolm land railroad work with the promise of free rides to New York. He started by loading food onto trains, then became a fourth cook, spending spare hours exploring downtown Washington, where Black hardship looked worse than anything in Roxbury. Visits to Upper Manhattan and places like Small's Paradise and the Apollo altered his sense of what was possible.

The people you surround yourself with determine your perspective about life and the choices you make.

At seventeen he lost the railroad job but found a role at Small's Paradise, listening to old-timers and talkative customers until he accumulated street knowledge across many subjects. His bright red dyed hair produced the nickname "Detroit Red." He moved into a rooming house, befriended sex workers there, and mingled with gamblers and criminals such as "Black Sammy," "Bub" Hewlett, "King," Padmore, and "West Indian Archie."

After mistakenly sending an undercover military agent to one of his hosts, he was fired and barred from Small's. With Sammy's help he sold marijuana in the music scene until narcotics officers closed in, profits collapsed, and he was reduced to begging for meals.

chapters.title: Detroit Red and the Hustler's Spiral
chapters.text:
Malcolm began exploiting others for a living, treating crime as retaliation for racism. Hard drugs entered his routine. He stopped trusting Sammy after a botched robbery nearly got Sammy shot; when Malcolm visited the next morning, Sammy's girlfriend was shouting about the incident. Malcolm struck her, Sammy pulled the trigger, and the girlfriend helped Malcolm escape. He forgave Sammy but never trusted him again.

Your environment has a direct effect on your behavior; so, surround yourself with good people.

West Indian Archie later threatened him over miscommunication about gambling profits — less about money than about publicly humiliating a younger hustler seeking stature. Archie spread the threat, and Malcolm nearly killed him. Drug dependence worsened until Sammy summoned Shorty from Boston to pull Malcolm out of Harlem.

chapters.title: Burglary, Prison, and the Road to Transformation
chapters.text:
Malcolm returned to Boston listless for a month, using drugs to numb worry while Sophia financed him. He lost money gambling at John Hughes's house and formed a burglary crew with Sophia, her sister, Shorty, and Rudy in Roxbury. The women entered wealthy homes to note valuables and sketch layouts; Malcolm and Shorty robbed them while Rudy drove. A stolen watch triggered alerts to detectives and jewelers, and everyone except Rudy was arrested.

At a low point in Malcolm Little's life, encouragement from a few people set him on a brand new course, proving that there is a silver lining in every cloud.

In 1946 he was sentenced to ten years at Charlestown State Prison. Early imprisonment meant drugs, rebellion, and the nickname "Satan." Inmate Bimbi and family letters nudged him toward education. Transfers to Concord and later to Norfolk's experimental rehabilitation prison introduced correspondence study and the Nation of Islam through relatives devoted to Elijah Muhammad, "The Messenger of Allah." Reginald wrote telling him to stop eating pork and smoking.

Looking back, I think I really was at least slightly out of my mind. I viewed narcotics as most people regard food. I wore my guns as today I wore my neckties. ~ Malcolm X

chapters.title: Books, Faith, and the Birth of Malcolm X
chapters.text:
Hilda urged Malcolm to write Elijah Muhammad, who answered with money enclosed. Prayer proved difficult — nearly a week passed before he learned the practice — yet writing to Muhammad, his siblings, and other Black contacts became a source of pride. Knowing his vocabulary was thin, he copied words from a dictionary onto tablets, read them aloud, and memorized meanings overnight. Reading expanded into Black history, slavery, and any text he thought useful to Black liberation. He also worked to record Muhammad's teachings.

For you to be passionate about a thing, there is a need for discipline and consistency.

Prison debates launched his public-speaking life. In 1952 Wilfred, his oldest brother, received him after release. Malcolm sold furniture, lived with family, practiced Islam at Detroit's Temple Number One, and received an "X" from Chicago headquarters — shedding "Little" and marking the African name slavery had erased. By 1953 he quit the furniture store for factory work and became assistant minister at Temple Number One.

chapters.title: Ministry, Marriage, and Rising Influence
chapters.text:
As Minister Malcolm X, he traveled to spread Elijah Muhammad's message and open temples. He served Temple Eleven in Boston, then in 1954 moved to Philadelphia and established Temple Twelve within three months. Success there led to Temple Seven in New York, where results were initially sparse. He printed leaflets, walked the streets with a small group of brothers, and approached Black Christians leaving Sunday services, urging them to hear Muhammad. Membership grew; temples followed in Springfield and Hartford.

The experiences of Malcolm X have proven to us that we can be a better version of ourselves if we live a purposeful life.

He helped launch Temple Fifteen in Atlanta in 1955. The Muslim community expanded in 1956 as affluent members became ministers. That year he developed feelings for Betty X, received Muhammad's blessing, married her on January 14, 1958, and eventually fathered six daughters. In 1959 Amsterdam News coverage, television, and books pushed the Nation into national view, and Malcolm began defending it in panels and debates.

Did you know? According to CNN, on 12th February 2021, Malcolm X's childhood home at Roxbury in Boston was officially recognized as a historic site.

chapters.title: Breakaway, Legacy, and Assassination
chapters.text:
As Malcolm's visibility rose, whispers claimed he wanted to seize the Nation of Islam. In 1962 leadership stopped highlighting his work because he had eclipsed Elijah Muhammad's profile. After enduring envy, jealousy, and betrayal, he broke away in 1964 while remaining Muslim. He formed Muslim Mosque, Inc. to challenge Black Americans to claim rights and heal spiritual, intellectual, political, and economic wounds. He also created the Organization of Afro-American Unity, a secular pan-Africanist group defending Black human rights and cooperation.

Hostility persisted. In February 1964 a bomb struck his car on orders from Temple Number Seven's leader, and Muhammad's speeches implied Malcolm should die. On February 21, 1965, assassins shot him at the Audubon Ballroom in Manhattan as he prepared to speak to the OAAU. He died shortly after at Columbia Presbyterian Hospital and was buried at Ferncliff Cemetery in Hartsdale, New York. Thomas Hagan, Norman 3X Butler, and Thomas 15X Johnson were convicted. OAAU and MMI folded after his death. A statue now marks the ballroom in Washington Heights.

Try this

Be deliberate about the friends you keep because they impact your life in the long run. Hang around people who are ambitious, focused, disciplined, and upright; you'll more than likely end up just like them.
